revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 八柱神社 |画像 = |所在地 = [[愛知県]][[碧南市]]弥生町3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 52|緯度秒 = 07.9 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 59|経度秒 = 20.0 |祭神 = [[アメノオシホミミ|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命]]
[[アマツヒコネ|天津彦根命]] |神体 = |社格 = 旧郷社 |創建 = 仁寿3年（853年）8月21日 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 10月第3日曜日 |神事 = |地図 = Japan Aichi }} '''八柱神社'''（やはしらじんしゃ）は、[[愛知県]][[碧南市]]弥生町3-140にある[[神社]]。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は郷社。仁寿3年（853年）8月21日創建。[[碧海郡]][[棚尾町]]の氏神だった。境外社として、1931年（昭和6年）に創建された[[護国神社]]の[[棚尾神社]]がある。 == 祭神 == * [[アメノオシホミミ|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命]]（あめのおしほみみのみこと） * [[アマツヒコネ|天津彦根命]]（あまつひこねのみこと） == 歴史 == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|境外社の[[棚尾神社]]]] 仁寿3年（853年）8月21日、志貴荘司・志貴左衛門藤原周亮が八王子宮を創建した[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm 八柱神社] トボトボ歩く碧南市。永歴元年（1160年）、[[尾張国|尾張]][[知多郡]][[内海町 (愛知県)|内海]]から来た長田白正が神主となり、以後は[[明治維新]]まで長田氏が社家を務めた。現在の大浜地区にある[[西方寺 (碧南市)|西方寺]]は、明応5年（1496年）まで八王子宮の境内にあり、光照寺と称していた。 文政10年（1827年）には、前浜新田を開発した斎藤倭助によって[[灯籠]]が建立された。1872年（明治5年）9月には八王子宮から八柱神社に改称し、[[近代社格制度]]における村社となった。1884年（明治17年）2月28日には郷社に昇格した。同年3月18日には国学者の[[山中信天]]が社号額を奉納している。 1919年（大正8年）9月には、[[矢作川]]に架かる棚尾橋の償却期終了記念として、境内に太鼓橋が建立された。1923年（大正12年）には大[[鳥居]]が建立された。1931年（昭和6年）10月18日には、境外社として[[護国神社]]の[[棚尾神社]]が創建された[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf 「棚尾神社と忠魂碑] 棚尾の歴史を語る会。 == 地理 == かつての[[碧海郡]][[棚尾町]]の集落の南端部にある。かつては[[碧海台地]]南西部の突端部に位置しており、神社の南側や西側には海（[[衣浦湾]]）が広がっていた。海岸には文禄年間（1592年-1596年）以後に[[塩田]]が形成され、塩浜町という町名に名残が残っている。ここで生産された塩は大浜塩として信濃国に運ばれ、[[にがり]]成分が多い特徴を生かして塩漬けなどに使用された。境内には井戸「弥生の井」があり、[[醸造業]]に適する水質だったことから、棚尾では醸造業が栄えた。 == 現地情報 == ; 所在地 * [[愛知県]][[碧南市]]弥生町3-140 ; 交通アクセス * 鉄道：[[名鉄三河線]][[碧南駅]]から徒歩15分 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ 八柱神社] 碧南観光ナビ * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm 八柱神社] トボトボ歩く碧南市 {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やはしらしんしや}} [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:碧南市の歴史]] [[Category:碧南市の建築物]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]]

[[アマツヒコネ|天津彦根命]] |神体 = |社格 = 旧郷社 |創建 = 仁寿3年（853年）8月21日 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 10月第3日曜日 |神事 = |地図 = Japan Aichi }} '''八柱神社'''（やはしらじんしゃ）は、[[愛知県]][[碧南市]]弥生町3-140にある[[神社]]。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は郷社。仁寿3年（853年）8月21日創建。[[碧海郡]][[棚尾町]]の氏神だった。境外社として、1931年（昭和6年）に創建された[[護国神社]]の[[棚尾神社]]がある。 == 祭神 == * [[アメノオシホミミ|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命]]（あめのおしほみみのみこと） * [[アマツヒコネ|天津彦根命]]（あまつひこねのみこと） == 歴史 == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|境外社の[[棚尾神社]]]] 仁寿3年（853年）8月21日、志貴荘司の志貴左衛門藤原周亮が八王子宮を創建した[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm 八柱神社] トボトボ歩く碧南市。永歴元年（1160年）、[[尾張国|尾張]][[知多郡]][[内海町 (愛知県)|内海]]から来た長田白正が神主となり、以後は[[明治維新]]まで長田氏が社家を務めた。現在の大浜地区にある[[西方寺 (碧南市)|西方寺]]は、明応5年（1496年）まで八王子宮の境内にあり、光照寺と称していた。 文政10年（1827年）には、前浜新田を開発した斎藤倭助によって[[灯籠]]が建立された。1872年（明治5年）9月には八王子宮から八柱神社に改称し、[[近代社格制度]]における村社となった。1884年（明治17年）2月28日には郷社に昇格した。同年3月18日には書家の[[山中信天翁]]が社号額を奉納している。 1919年（大正8年）9月には、[[矢作川]]に架かる棚尾橋の償却期終了記念として、境内に太鼓橋が建立された。1923年（大正12年）には大[[鳥居]]が建立された。1931年（昭和6年）10月18日には、境外社として[[護国神社]]の[[棚尾神社]]が創建された[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf 「棚尾神社と忠魂碑] 棚尾の歴史を語る会。八柱神社近辺には造り酒屋の旦那衆に支えられて花街が形成されており、劇場[[三栄座]]の開館によってこの花街も発展した。 == 地理 == かつての[[碧海郡]][[棚尾町]]の集落の南端部にある。かつては[[碧海台地]]南西部の突端部に位置しており、神社の南側や西側には海（[[衣浦湾]]）が広がっていた。海岸には文禄年間（1592年-1596年）以後に[[塩田]]が形成され、塩浜町という町名に名残が残っている。ここで生産された塩は大浜塩として信濃国に運ばれ、[[にがり]]成分が多い特徴を生かして塩漬けなどに使用された。境内には井戸「弥生の井」があり、[[醸造業]]に適する水質だったことから、棚尾では醸造業が栄えた。 == 現地情報 == ; 所在地 * [[愛知県]][[碧南市]]弥生町3-140 ; 交通アクセス * 鉄道：[[名鉄三河線]][[碧南駅]]から徒歩15分 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 碧南事典編さん会「八柱神社」『碧南事典』碧南市、1993年、469頁 == 外部リンク == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ 八柱神社] 碧南観光ナビ * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm 八柱神社] トボトボ歩く碧南市 {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やはしらしんしや}} [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:碧南市の歴史]] [[Category:碧南市の建築物]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name = Yahashira Shrine |Image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |Latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |Longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |Deities = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |Rank = Former Gōsha |Founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |Festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |Map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. Founded on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}]] The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Palace|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around Hekinan City {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Hekinan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Hekinan]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Japan]] [[ja:八柱神社]]

In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around Hekinan City {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Hekinan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Hekinan]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Original pages]][[ja:八柱神社 (碧南市)]] [[ja:八柱神社]]

{{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. Founded on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}]] The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around Hekinan City {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:History of Hekinan]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Buildings and structures in Hekinan]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Japan]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Original pages]][[ja:八柱神社 (碧南市)]] [[ja:八柱神社]]

The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. Founded on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}} The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around Hekinan City {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:History of Hekinan]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Hekinan]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:八柱神社 (碧南市)]] [[ja:八柱神社]]

The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. {{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. Founded on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}} The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around Hekinan City {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:History of Hekinan]] [[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Hekinan]] [[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Japan]] [[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yahashira Shrine (Hekinan)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[ja:八柱神社 (碧南市)]] [[ja:八柱神社]]

In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. Founded on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}} The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. Founded on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}} The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. Founded on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}} The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. Founded on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}} The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. Founded on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}} The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around revision.comment: adding religious affiliation to the ones with a broken religious affiliation section
revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. consecration_year on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}} The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. consecration_year on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}]] The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * {{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}} was Gōsha. consecration_year on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}]] The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}} was Gōsha. consecration_year on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}]] The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * {{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}} was Gōsha. consecration_year on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}]] The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * {{Shrine |name = Yahashira Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}} was Gōsha. consecration_year on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}]]] The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yahashira Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tanao Yahashira-jinja ac (1).jpg|300px]] |location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Yayoi-chō (Hekinan)|ja|弥生町 (碧南市)}} 3-140 |ISO = JP-23 |latitude = 34|LatMin = 52|LatSec = 07.9 |longitude = 136|LonMin = 59|LonSec = 20.0 |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}}
{{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} |SacredObjects = |type= Former Gōsha |founded = {{ill|Ninju 3|ja|仁寿}} (853), August 21 |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |festival = Third Sunday in October |Rites = |map = Japan Aichi }} '''Yahashira Shrine''' (八柱神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located at 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}} was Gōsha. consecration_year on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), it served as the guardian shrine of the Tanao community in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}. Its auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}}, dedicated to the spirits of war dead, was established in 1931. == Deities == * {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|ja|正哉吾勝勝速日天忍穂耳命}} (Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto) * {{ill|Amatsuhikone|ja|天津彦根命}} (Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto) == History == [[File:Tanao-jinja-with-torii.jpg|thumb|Auxiliary shrine, {{ill|Tanao Gokoku Shrine|ja|棚尾神社}]]] The shrine was established on August 21, 853 (Ninju 3), by Shiki Saemon Fujiwara Norisuke, who constructed the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子宮}} on the site.[http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine], "Walking Around Hekinan City." In 1160 (Eiryaku 1), Nagata Shosei from {{ill|Utsumi|ja|内海町 (愛知県)}} in {{ill|Chita District|ja|知多郡}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}, became the head priest. The Nagata family served as shrine priests until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. The nearby {{ill|Saihō-ji Temple (Hekinan)|ja|西方寺 (碧南市)}}, originally located within the shrine grounds, was known as Kōshō-ji until 1496 (Meiō 5). Other historical landmarks include a stone lantern erected in 1827 (Bunsei 10) by Saitō Wasuke, who developed the Maehama New Fields. The shrine was renamed Yahashira Shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5) and elevated to Gōsha rank in 1884 (Meiji 17). In the same year, a shrine plaque was donated by the calligrapher {{ill|Yamanaka Shintenō|ja|山中信天翁}}. In 1919 (Taishō 8), a taiko bridge was built to commemorate the repayment of the debt for Tanao Bridge over the {{ill|Yahagi River|ja|矢作川}}. Other notable additions include a large torii in 1923 (Taishō 12) and the establishment of Tanao Shrine as an auxiliary shrine in 1931 (Shōwa 6).[http://www.city.hekinan.lg.jp/material/files/group/7/katarukai20.pdf "Tanao Shrine and the Monument to Loyal Spirits"], "Telling the History of Tanao." == Geography == The shrine is situated in the southern part of the former Tanao village in {{ill|Hekikai District|ja|碧海郡}}, near the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Hekikai Plateau|ja|碧海台地}}. Historically, it overlooked the sea, with {{ill|Enokura Bay|ja|衣浦湾}} stretching to its south and west. Salt fields were developed along the coast during the Bunroku era (1592–1596), leaving the name Shiobama-chō ("Salt Beach Town") as a legacy. Salt produced here was exported to {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and used for pickling, thanks to its high brine content. The shrine grounds include a well known as "Yayoi Well," whose water quality contributed to the flourishing of the brewing industry in Tanao. == Visitor Information == ; Address * 3-140 Yayoi-chō, {{ill|Hekinan|ja|碧南市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}} ; Access * By train: 15-minute walk from {{ill|Hekinan Station|ja|碧南駅}} on the {{ill|Meitetsu Mikawa Line|ja|名鉄三河線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Hekinan City Encyclopedia Committee, "Yahashira Shrine," ''Hekinan Encyclopedia,'' Hekinan City, 1993, p. 469. == External Links == * [https://www.hekinan-kanko.jp/highlight/detail/100/ Yahashira Shrine] - Hekinan Tourism Guide * [http://www.katch.ne.jp/~hiro32/machi_info/tanao_area/tanao04.htm Yahashira Shrine] - Walking Around
